mnemonics.title: Fragmentation Types
mnemonics.content: INTERNAL: INSIDE THE BOX - WASTE INSIDE ALLOCATED MEMORY. EXTERNAL: ESCAPES THE BOX - WASTE OUTSIDE AS SCATTERED HOLES.

mnemonics.title: Fit Algorithms
mnemonics.content: FIRST: FIND FIRST SUITABLE. BEST: FIND SMALLEST SUITABLE. WORST: FIND LARGEST SUITABLE. NEXT: RESUME FROM LAST POSITION.

mnemonics.title: Base and Limit Registers
mnemonics.content: BASE BEGINS - STARTING ADDRESS. LIMIT LIMITS - PROCESS SIZE. BASE FOR ADDRESSING, LIMIT FOR PROTECTING.

acronyms.expansion: First Fit - Allocate first hole large enough
acronyms.usage: Used when speed is important and memory is not severely fragmented

acronyms.expansion: Best Fit - Allocate smallest hole large enough
acronyms.usage: Minimizes wasted space but requires searching all holes

acronyms.expansion: Worst Fit - Allocate largest available hole
acronyms.usage: Leaves large remaining holes, good for varied workload

acronyms.expansion: Next Fit - Continue search from last allocation
acronyms.usage: Faster than first fit for multiple allocations, similar fragmentation

acronyms.expansion: Memory Management Unit - Hardware for address translation
acronyms.usage: Hardware component that uses base/limit registers for address translation

visualTips.visualization: Draw memory as a long horizontal bar. Fixed partitions show equal vertical lines dividing it. Variable partitions show blocks of varying widths with gaps (holes) between them.

Internal: Draw a box representing a partition, shade the used portion, leave unshaded empty corner inside - that is wasted space INSIDE. External: Draw several small empty rectangles scattered between filled rectangles - these cannot be combined easily.
Draw two number lines - top is logical addresses (0 to limit-1), bottom is physical addresses (base to base+limit). Show arrows connecting each logical address to physical by adding the base value.
Show memory as blocks with Process A, Hole, Process B, Hole, Process C. Then show arrows moving all processes together, combining holes into one big Hole.

analogies.analogy: Like assigning a whole floor of a building to one department - the department gets all rooms from floor start to end. Cannot split across floors (non-contiguous).

Like parking cars in a parking lot where some cars leave, creating scattered empty spots. A large truck needing 5 consecutive spots cannot park even though 5 spots total are free because they're not adjacent.
Like a person renting an apartment in a building with fixed room sizes. If they need only a small room but the smallest available is much larger, they pay for unused space inside their apartment.
Like reorganizing furniture in a room to push all empty space to one corner so you can place a large new item that needs continuous space.

Like a book with page numbers starting from 1, but the book is placed at a certain page in a larger notebook. To find the real page, you add the starting page number to the book's internal page number.
